name: Pipes and Cisterns — Filling and Leaking
whatIsIt: Pipes-and-cisterns is time-and-work in disguise: a tank is the 'job'. An inlet pipe does POSITIVE work (fills part of the tank each hour); an outlet pipe or a leak does NEGATIVE work (empties part each hour). Add the rates with their signs to get the net rate, then divide the tank by the net rate.
whyNeeded: Water tanks, fuel tanks, reservoirs, and even 'money in vs money out' budgeting are pipes-and-cisterns problems. It is a guaranteed topic in SSC, Bank and Railway aptitude papers, and the sign trick (fill +, leak −) makes even tricky leak problems quick.
realLifeUses: A tap filling a bucket while a small hole leaks water out
An overhead tank with two inlet pipes and one outlet
A petrol tank filling while the engine draws fuel
A reservoir: rain inflow vs irrigation outflow
SSC / Bank / Railway quantitative aptitude questions

vocabulary.meaning: Fills the tank — a positive rate (part of the tank per hour).
Empties the tank — a negative rate (subtract it).
Sum of all rates with signs; positive net fills, negative net empties.
Set tank capacity = LCM of the pipe times so each rate is a whole number.

story.title: The Tap and the Hidden Leak
story.text: Ravi's tap fills the water drum in 6 hours. But there's a small leak that would empty a full drum in 12 hours. 'So how long to fill it now?' Grandmother says: 'Make the drum 12 units — that's the LCM. The tap adds 12 ÷ 6 = 2 units an hour; the leak removes 12 ÷ 12 = 1 unit an hour. Net = 2 − 1 = 1 unit an hour, so filling the 12-unit drum takes 12 hours instead of 6.' Ravi finally understands why the drum takes so long.
story.extractedProblem: A tap fills a drum in 6 hours; a leak empties it in 12 hours. With both acting, how long to fill?
story.answerInStory: 12 hours — net rate is 2 − 1 = 1 unit/hour on a 12-unit drum.

visual.caption: Fill is positive, leak is negative. Add the signed rates for the net rate, then divide the capacity by it.

teachingGallery.title: Fill +, leak −, then divide
teachingGallery.note: Capacity = LCM of the times. Inlet rate is positive, outlet/leak rate is negative. Time = capacity ÷ net rate.

standardMethod.summary: Capacity = LCM of the pipe times; add signed rates (fill +, empty −); divide capacity by net rate.
steps: Set tank capacity = LCM of all the given times.
Inlet rate = capacity ÷ its fill time (positive).
Outlet/leak rate = capacity ÷ its empty time (negative).
Net rate = sum of signed rates; time = capacity ÷ net rate.

alternateMethods.name: Fraction method
steps: Fill in 1/a per hour, empty in 1/b per hour.

workedExamples.problem: Pipe A fills a tank in 4 hours, pipe B in 6 hours. Both open — how long to fill?
A pipe fills a cistern in 5 hours; a waste pipe empties it in 10 hours. Both open, how long to fill?

trickPractice.intro: Capacity = LCM; add inlets, subtract outlets; divide.

questions.q: Fill in 3h, fill in 6h (both taps). Hours to fill?
Fill in 4h, leak empties in 8h. Hours to fill?
Fill in 2h, fill in 2h (both taps). Hours to fill?
Fill in 6h, leak empties in 18h. Hours to fill?

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding the leak's rate instead of subtracting it, so the tank seems to fill faster with a leak.
commonMistakes.fix: A leak fights the fill — its rate is NEGATIVE. Subtract it. If the net rate ends up negative, the tank can never fill (it empties).

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding the fill times (6 + 12 = 18) to combine pipes.
commonMistakes.fix: Combine RATES, not times. Two inlets together fill faster than either alone, so the time must drop below the fastest pipe.

easy.q: Two taps fill a tank in 6h and 3h. Both open — hours to fill?
A tank fills in 5h but a leak empties it in 10h. With both, filling will be…
hintLadder: It must take longer than 5 hours (it's 10).

medium.q: Fill in 12h; leak empties in 24h. Hours to fill with both?
Pipes fill in 10h and 15h. Both open — hours to fill?

challenge.q: Two inlets fill in 6h and 12h; one outlet empties in 8h. All open — hours to fill?
The net rate of a tank system comes out NEGATIVE. What happens?

options.label: The tank empties and never fills

challenge.answer: The tank empties and never fills
hintLadder: Negative net = more out than in.

questions.q: Taps fill in 4h and 4h. Both open — hours to fill?
Fill in 8h; leak empties in 24h. Hours to fill?
How do you combine an inlet and an outlet pipe?

options.label: Subtract the outlet's rate from the inlet's

questions.answer: Subtract the outlet's rate from the inlet's

questions.q: Pipes fill in 9h and 18h. Both open — hours to fill?
Two inlets, times 6h and 12h. The joint fill time is…

teachBackPrompt: Teach someone the sign rule for pipes: inlets are +, outlets/leaks are −. Use a tank that fills in 6h and leaks empty in 12h, make it 12 units, and show why the net rate is +1 and it takes 12 hours.

revisionCard.summary: Pipes = time-and-work with signs. Capacity = LCM of times. Inlet rate +, outlet/leak rate −. Net rate = signed sum; time = capacity ÷ net. Negative net ⇒ empties, never fills. Two inlets always beat the fastest single pipe.

gameMission.title: Fill the Tank, Beat the Leak
gameMission.brief: Open the right pipes, subtract the sneaky leaks, and hit the fill line before time runs out. Spot the traps where a leak turns the net rate negative!

realLifeProject: Fill a bucket from a tap while it drips from a tiny hole (or imagine one). Estimate the fill rate and the leak rate in 'buckets per minute', subtract, and predict the fill time — then check with a real timer.

rememberIt.unpack: Capacity = LCM. Net rate = inlets − outlets. Time = capacity ÷ net. Negative net means it empties.

formulas.name: Net rate
formulas.whenToUse: Any mix of fill and empty pipes.

studentQuestions.q: What if the leak is faster than the tap?
studentQuestions.a: Then the net rate is negative — more water leaves than enters, so the tank empties and can never fill.

studentQuestions.q: Is this really the same as time-and-work?
studentQuestions.a: Yes! The tank is the job, pipes are workers. The only new idea is that outlets do negative work.
